chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Isostructural with calcite (trigonal R-3c). Zn2+ occupies the divalent cation site. Forms solid solutions toward siderite (Fe), rhodochrosite (Mn), and otavite (Cd).
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Color is determined by trace substitution: Co gives blue-lavender (cobaltian smithsonite), Cu gives green or teal-blue (cuprian smithsonite), Mn gives pink, Fe gives yellow-brown, Cd gives colorless to pale yellow. Pure ZnCO3 is white to colorless. Each color variant has its own collector following.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: Rarely forms well-developed crystals; typically botryoidal, stalactitic, massive, or as crusts with rounded surfaces. Botryoidal form with translucent pearl-like surfaces is classic and most recognizable. Earthy and cellular forms also occur.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Notably heavy for a non-metallic mineral (SG 4.3–4.5) due to Zn. The pearly botryoidal surfaces are lustrous and translucent, giving an almost ceramic quality. Effervesces in dilute HCl.

color_profiles.name: Cobaltian Smithsonite (Blue / Lavender)
color_profiles.conditions: Tsumeb, Namibia; Laurion, Greece; Kelly Mine, New Mexico.

color_profiles.name: Cuprian Smithsonite (Green / Teal)
color_profiles.conditions: Arizona, New Mexico deposits; any Cu-rich zinc deposit oxidation zone.

color_profiles.name: White / Gray Smithsonite
color_profiles.cause: Near-pure ZnCO3 with minimal chromophore substitution.

color_profiles.name: Yellow / Brown Smithsonite
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Fe3+ substitution or iron oxide staining.

appearance.visual_notes: Smithsonite is primarily known as a secondary zinc carbonate with exceptional color range and unusual pearlescent botryoidal habit. Cobalt-bearing blue smithsonite from Tsumeb and copper-bearing green from Arizona are collector favorites. The pearly luster of botryoidal forms is distinctive.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce pale blue, green, or white under UV
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.621–1.849 (high birefringence like other calcite-group members).

geologic_environments: oxidized zones of sphalerite-bearing polymetallic sulfide deposits
fractures and cavities in limestone above zinc ore
host_rocks: any host rock in oxidized zone of zinc deposit
notable_localities: Kelly Mine, Socorro County, New Mexico, USA (cobaltian lavender-blue)
Broken Hill, New South Wales, Australia
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Classic zinc supergene mineral. Forms when oxidizing groundwaters rich in CO2 react with sphalerite (ZnS) and zinc-bearing fluids in carbonate host environments. Named after James Smithson, the British mineralogist and founder of the Smithsonian Institution (who also studied zinc minerals).

historical_uses: Zinc ore (minor source in oxidized deposits)
Gem and lapidary use (cabochons from colored botryoidal material)
cultural_associations: Named after James Smithson (1765–1829) — founder of the Smithsonian Institution
Smithsonite specimens formed part of Smithson's own mineral collection
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Smithsonite is named for James Smithson — chemist, mineralogist, and benefactor of the Smithsonian Institution. He actually studied calamine (a historical term covering hemimorphite and smithsonite). The mineral was formally named for him posthumously by François Sulpice Beudant in 1832.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Smithsonite is quiet but extraordinary — a carbonate that refracts light through translucent botryoidal skins in a dozen colors. The cobalt-blue variety from Tsumeb has a quality like polished sky.

practical_notes.toxicity: Zinc is an essential trace element; high concentrations in dust form warrants caution. Cadmium substitution in Cd-rich smithsonite specimens warrants extra care.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Relatively brittle; handle botryoidal masses carefully. Soft surfaces can be marred.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid acids. Clean with dry cloth. Do not soak in water. Store away from harder minerals.
treatments_or_synthetics: May be stabilized with resin for lapidary use
common_confusions: Hemimorphite (zinc silicate — harder; different crystal habit)
practical_notes.market_notes: Colored specimens (cobalt-blue, green, lavender) command collector premiums. White/gray masses are inexpensive. Lapidary-quality botryoidal material moderately priced.

prompt_fragments: pearlescent botryoidal smithsonite domes — periwinkle blue cobalt
apple-green cuprian smithsonite over gossan matrix
secondary zinc carbonate translucent skin on limestone
